name: Cuneiform (Sumerian / Akkadian)
approx_period: c. 3200 BCE – 75 CE (latest known dated cuneiform tablet: 75 CE astronomical text)
extant_corpus_size: ~500,000+ clay tablets and fragments; major collections at the British Museum (~130,000), Yale Babylonian Collection (~45,000), and Vorderasiatisches Museum Berlin
key_source_documents: Rawlinson, H.C. (1851), 'Outline of the History of Assyria', JRAS 12 — first systematic decipherment of Akkadian cuneiform
Behistun Inscription (Bisotun, Iran), c. 520 BCE — trilingual (Old Persian / Elamite / Babylonian Akkadian), key to decipherment
Grotefend, G.F. (1802), Preis-Schrift — first successful reading of Old Persian cuneiform wedge-signs
Standard Assyrian Dictionary (CAD), Oriental Institute, Chicago, 1956–2010 — definitive Akkadian lexicon
Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature (ETCSL), University of Oxford — primary Sumerian literary corpus
visual_characteristics: Cuneiform signs are impressed into wet clay tablets using a reed stylus cut at an angle, producing characteristic wedge-shaped (Latin: cuneus) impressions — horizontal, vertical, and diagonal strokes combined into complex sign-clusters. Early Uruk-period signs (c. 3200 BCE) are more pictographic and written in vertical columns; by the Early Dynastic period (c. 2900 BCE) tablets rotate 90° and signs are read left-to-right in horizontal rows. Late Babylonian script is highly cursive and compressed. Sign counts range from ~600 common signs (Old Babylonian) to ~1,000+ in the full sign repertoire.
notes: Covers both Sumerian (language isolate) and Akkadian (Semitic) uses of the shared cuneiform script system. Episode T3_dryrun references Sumerian. See also proto-writing entry pw_cuneiform_uruk_proto for precursor token/proto-sign system.
